question:
2. Suppose that each set \( X \) of points in the plane has an associated set \( \bar{X} \) of points called its cover. Suppose further that
(1) \( \bar{X} \cup Y \overline{\bar{X}} \cup \bar{Y} \cup Y \), where \( \cup \) designates point set sum (or union) and \( \supset \) denotes set inclusion.
Ptove: (i) \( \bar{X} \supset X \), (ii) \( \overline{\bar{X}}=\bar{X} \), (iii) \( X \supset Y \) implies \( \bar{X} \supset \bar{Y} \).
Prove conversely that (i), (ii) and (iii) imply (1).
solution:
Solution. In (1) let \( Y=X \) : then
\[
\bar{X} \supset \overline{\bar{X}} \cup \bar{X} \cup X
\]
from which it is clear that \( \bar{X} \supset X \), which is (i).
We note from the above that \( \bar{X} \supset \overline{\bar{X}} \). In (i) we replace \( X \) by \( \bar{X} \) to get \( \overline{\bar{X}} \supset \bar{X} \). These two relations imply that \( \overline{\bar{X}}=\bar{X} \), which is (ii).

Suppose \( Y \subset X \). Then \( X \cup Y=X \), and (1) reduces to
\[
\bar{X} \supset \overline{\bar{X}} \cup \bar{Y} \cup Y
\]
whence \( \bar{X} \supset \bar{Y} \), which is (iii).
Now suppose that (i), (ii), and (iii) hold. For any sets \( X \) and \( Y \) we have
\[
X \subset X \cup Y .
\]

By (iii),
\[
\bar{X} \subset \bar{X} \cup \bar{Y} .
\]

Hence by (ii)
\[
\overline{\bar{X}} \subset \bar{X} \cup \bar{Y} .
\]

Also
\[
Y \subset X \cup Y
\]
and by (iii)
\[
\bar{Y} \subset \bar{X} \cup Y .
\]

From (i)
\[
X \cup Y \subset \bar{X} \cup \bar{Y}
\]
so
\[
Y \subset \overline{X \cup Y} .
\]

Now (2), (3) and (4) together yield
\[
\overline{X \cup Y} \supset \overline{\bar{X}} \cup \bar{Y} \cup Y,
\]
which is the required condition (1).
Remark. See Garrett Birkhoff, Lattice Theory, Amer. Math. Soc. Colloquium Pub., vol. 25, Providence, R.I., 1967, page 113.

descriptive_long.question:
2. Suppose that each set \( firstset \) of points in the plane has an associated set \( \bar{firstset} \) of points called its cover. Suppose further that
(1) \( \bar{firstset} \cup secondset \overline{\bar{firstset}} \cup \bar{secondset} \cup secondset \), where \( \cup \) designates point set sum (or union) and \( \supset \) denotes set inclusion.
Ptove: (i) \( \bar{firstset} \supset firstset \), (ii) \( \overline{\bar{firstset}}=\bar{firstset} \), (iii) \( firstset \supset secondset \) implies \( \bar{firstset} \supset \bar{secondset} \).
Prove conversely that (i), (ii) and (iii) imply (1).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. In (1) let \( secondset=firstset \) : then
\[
\bar{firstset} \supset \overline{\bar{firstset}} \cup \bar{firstset} \cup firstset
\]
from which it is clear that \( \bar{firstset} \supset firstset \), which is (i).
We note from the above that \( \bar{firstset} \supset \overline{\bar{firstset}} \). In (i) we replace \( firstset \) by \( \bar{firstset} \) to get \( \overline{\bar{firstset}} \supset \bar{firstset} \). These two relations imply that \( \overline{\bar{firstset}}=\bar{firstset} \), which is (ii).

Suppose \( secondset \subset firstset \). Then \( firstset \cup secondset=firstset \), and (1) reduces to
\[
\bar{firstset} \supset \overline{\bar{firstset}} \cup \bar{secondset} \cup secondset
\]
whence \( \bar{firstset} \supset \bar{secondset} \), which is (iii).
Now suppose that (i), (ii), and (iii) hold. For any sets \( firstset \) and \( secondset \) we have
\[
firstset \subset firstset \cup secondset .
\]

By (iii),
\[
\bar{firstset} \subset \bar{firstset} \cup \bar{secondset} .
\]

Hence by (ii)
\[
\overline{\bar{firstset}} \subset \bar{firstset} \cup \bar{secondset} .
\]

Also
\[
secondset \subset firstset \cup secondset
\]
and by (iii)
\[
\bar{secondset} \subset \bar{firstset} \cup secondset .
\]

From (i)
\[
firstset \cup secondset \subset \bar{firstset} \cup \bar{secondset}
\]
so
\[
secondset \subset \overline{firstset \cup secondset} .
\]

Now (2), (3) and (4) together yield
\[
\overline{firstset \cup secondset} \supset \overline{\bar{firstset}} \cup \bar{secondset} \cup secondset,
\]
which is the required condition (1).
Remark. See Garrett Birkhoff, Lattice Theory, Amer. Math. Soc. Colloquium Pub., vol. 25, Providence, R.I., 1967, page 113.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
2. Suppose that each set \( perimeter \) of points in the plane has an associated set \( \bar{perimeter} \) of points called its cover. Suppose further that
(1) \( \bar{perimeter} \cup lighthouse \overline{\bar{perimeter}} \cup \bar{lighthouse} \cup lighthouse \), where \( \cup \) designates point set sum (or union) and \( \supset \) denotes set inclusion.
Ptove: (i) \( \bar{perimeter} \supset perimeter \), (ii) \( \overline{\bar{perimeter}}=\bar{perimeter} \), (iii) \( perimeter \supset lighthouse \) implies \( \bar{perimeter} \supset \bar{lighthouse} \).
Prove conversely that (i), (ii) and (iii) imply (1).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. In (1) let \( lighthouse=perimeter \) : then
\[
\bar{perimeter} \supset \overline{\bar{perimeter}} \cup \bar{perimeter} \cup perimeter
\]
from which it is clear that \( \bar{perimeter} \supset perimeter \), which is (i).
We note from the above that \( \bar{perimeter} \supset \overline{\bar{perimeter}} \). In (i) we replace \( perimeter \) by \( \bar{perimeter} \) to get \( \overline{\bar{perimeter}} \supset \bar{perimeter} \). These two relations imply that \( \overline{\bar{perimeter}}=\bar{perimeter} \), which is (ii).

Suppose \( lighthouse \subset perimeter \). Then \( perimeter \cup lighthouse=perimeter \), and (1) reduces to
\[
\bar{perimeter} \supset \overline{\bar{perimeter}} \cup \bar{lighthouse} \cup lighthouse
\]
whence \( \bar{perimeter} \supset \bar{lighthouse} \), which is (iii).
Now suppose that (i), (ii), and (iii) hold. For any sets \( perimeter \) and \( lighthouse \) we have
\[
perimeter \subset perimeter \cup lighthouse .
\]

By (iii),
\[
\bar{perimeter} \subset \bar{perimeter} \cup \bar{lighthouse} .
\]

Hence by (ii)
\[
\overline{\bar{perimeter}} \subset \bar{perimeter} \cup \bar{lighthouse} .
\]

Also
\[
lighthouse \subset perimeter \cup lighthouse
\]
and by (iii)
\[
\bar{lighthouse} \subset \bar{perimeter} \cup lighthouse .
\]

From (i)
\[
perimeter \cup lighthouse \subset \bar{perimeter} \cup \bar{lighthouse}
\]
so
\[
lighthouse \subset \overline{perimeter \cup lighthouse} .
\]

Now (2), (3) and (4) together yield
\[
\overline{perimeter \cup lighthouse} \supset \overline{\bar{perimeter}} \cup \bar{lighthouse} \cup lighthouse,
\]
which is the required condition (1).
Remark. See Garrett Birkhoff, Lattice Theory, Amer. Math. Soc. Colloquium Pub., vol. 25, Providence, R.I., 1967, page 113.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
2. Suppose that each set \( knownset \) of points in the plane has an associated set \( \bar{knownset} \) of points called its cover. Suppose further that
(1) \( \bar{knownset} \cup fixedset \overline{\bar{knownset}} \cup \bar{fixedset} \cup fixedset \), where \( \cup \) designates point set sum (or union) and \( \supset \) denotes set inclusion.
Ptove: (i) \( \bar{knownset} \supset knownset \), (ii) \( \overline{\bar{knownset}}=\bar{knownset} \), (iii) \( knownset \supset fixedset \) implies \( \bar{knownset} \supset \bar{fixedset} \).
Prove conversely that (i), (ii) and (iii) imply (1).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. In (1) let \( fixedset=knownset \) : then
\[
\bar{knownset} \supset \overline{\bar{knownset}} \cup \bar{knownset} \cup knownset
\]
from which it is clear that \( \bar{knownset} \supset knownset \), which is (i).
We note from the above that \( \bar{knownset} \supset \overline{\bar{knownset}} \). In (i) we replace \( knownset \) by \( \bar{knownset} \) to get \( \overline{\bar{knownset}} \supset \bar{knownset} \). These two relations imply that \( \overline{\bar{knownset}}=\bar{knownset} \), which is (ii).

Suppose \( fixedset \subset knownset \). Then \( knownset \cup fixedset=knownset \), and (1) reduces to
\[
\bar{knownset} \supset \overline{\bar{knownset}} \cup \bar{fixedset} \cup fixedset
\]
whence \( \bar{knownset} \supset \bar{fixedset} \), which is (iii).
Now suppose that (i), (ii), and (iii) hold. For any sets \( knownset \) and \( fixedset \) we have
\[
knownset \subset knownset \cup fixedset .
\]

By (iii),
\[
\bar{knownset} \subset \bar{knownset} \cup \bar{fixedset} .
\]

Hence by (ii)
\[
\overline{\bar{knownset}} \subset \bar{knownset} \cup \bar{fixedset} .
\]

Also
\[
fixedset \subset knownset \cup fixedset
\]
and by (iii)
\[
\bar{fixedset} \subset \bar{knownset} \cup fixedset .
\]

From (i)
\[
knownset \cup fixedset \subset \bar{knownset} \cup \bar{fixedset}
\]
so
\[
fixedset \subset \overline{knownset \cup fixedset} .
\]

Now (2), (3) and (4) together yield
\[
\overline{knownset \cup fixedset} \supset \overline{\bar{knownset}} \cup \bar{fixedset} \cup fixedset,
\]
which is the required condition (1).
Remark. See Garrett Birkhoff, Lattice Theory, Amer. Math. Soc. Colloquium Pub., vol. 25, Providence, R.I., 1967, page 113.

garbled_string.question:
2. Suppose that each set \( qzxwvtnp \) of points in the plane has an associated set \( \bar{qzxwvtnp} \) of points called its cover. Suppose further that
(1) \( \bar{qzxwvtnp} \cup hjgrksla \overline{\bar{qzxwvtnp}} \cup \bar{hjgrksla} \cup hjgrksla \), where \( \cup \) designates point set sum (or union) and \( \supset \) denotes set inclusion.
Ptove: (i) \( \bar{qzxwvtnp} \supset qzxwvtnp \), (ii) \( \overline{\bar{qzxwvtnp}}=\bar{qzxwvtnp} \), (iii) \( qzxwvtnp \supset hjgrksla \) implies \( \bar{qzxwvtnp} \supset \bar{hjgrksla} \).
Prove conversely that (i), (ii) and (iii) imply (1).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. In (1) let \( hjgrksla=qzxwvtnp \) : then
\[
\bar{qzxwvtnp} \supset \overline{\bar{qzxwvtnp}} \cup \bar{qzxwvtnp} \cup qzxwvtnp
\]
from which it is clear that \( \bar{qzxwvtnp} \supset qzxwvtnp \), which is (i).
We note from the above that \( \bar{qzxwvtnp} \supset \overline{\bar{qzxwvtnp}} \). In (i) we replace \( qzxwvtnp \) by \( \bar{qzxwvtnp} \) to get \( \overline{\bar{qzxwvtnp}} \supset \bar{qzxwvtnp} \). These two relations imply that \( \overline{\bar{qzxwvtnp}}=\bar{qzxwvtnp} \), which is (ii).

Suppose \( hjgrksla \subset qzxwvtnp \). Then \( qzxwvtnp \cup hjgrksla=qzxwvtnp \), and (1) reduces to
\[
\bar{qzxwvtnp} \supset \overline{\bar{qzxwvtnp}} \cup \bar{hjgrksla} \cup hjgrksla
\]
whence \( \bar{qzxwvtnp} \supset \bar{hjgrksla} \), which is (iii).
Now suppose that (i), (ii), and (iii) hold. For any sets \( qzxwvtnp \) and \( hjgrksla \) we have
\[
qzxwvtnp \subset qzxwvtnp \cup hjgrksla .
\]

By (iii),
\[
\bar{qzxwvtnp} \subset \bar{qzxwvtnp} \cup \bar{hjgrksla} .
\]

Hence by (ii)
\[
\overline{\bar{qzxwvtnp}} \subset \bar{qzxwvtnp} \cup \bar{hjgrksla} .
\]

Also
\[
hjgrksla \subset qzxwvtnp \cup hjgrksla
\]
and by (iii)
\[
\bar{hjgrksla} \subset \bar{qzxwvtnp} \cup hjgrksla .
\]

From (i)
\[
qzxwvtnp \cup hjgrksla \subset \bar{qzxwvtnp} \cup \bar{hjgrksla}
\]
so
\[
hjgrksla \subset \overline{qzxwvtnp \cup hjgrksla} .
\]

Now (2), (3) and (4) together yield
\[
\overline{qzxwvtnp \cup hjgrksla} \supset \overline{\bar{qzxwvtnp}} \cup \bar{hjgrksla} \cup hjgrksla,
\]
which is the required condition (1).
Remark. See Garrett Birkhoff, Lattice Theory, Amer. Math. Soc. Colloquium Pub., vol. 25, Providence, R.I., 1967, page 113.

kernel_variant.question:
Let S be a non-empty set.  
To every subset A \subseteq  S assign another subset, denoted A\star  and called the cloak of A.  
Assume that for every triple of subsets A, B, C \subseteq  S the following single axiom (the ``triple-cloak inclusion'') holds:

(1)  (A\star )\star  \cup  B\star  \cup  C \subseteq  (A \cup  B \cup  C)\star .

Prove that the cloak operation satisfies  
(i) Inflation    A \subseteq  A\star ;  
(ii) Idempotence  (A\star )\star  = A\star ;  
(iii) Monotonicity  A \subseteq  B \Rightarrow  A\star  \subseteq  B\star .  

Conversely, show that conditions (i)-(iii) alone force the triple-cloak inclusion (1).
kernel_variant.solution:
Forward direction: (1) \Rightarrow  (i)-(iii).  
------------------------------------------------
(i) Inflation.  
Put B = C = A in (1):

 (A\star )\star  \cup  A\star  \cup  A \subseteq  (A \cup  A \cup  A)\star  = A\star .

Because the right-hand side contains the whole left-hand side, in particular it contains A; hence A \subseteq  A\star .

(ii) Idempotence.  
With B = C = A again we obtained

 (A\star )\star  \cup  A\star  \cup  A \subseteq  A\star .  (2)

The right-hand side obviously contains A\star ; therefore (2) gives (A\star )\star  \subseteq  A\star .  
On the other hand, plugging A \leftarrow  A\star  (and keeping B = C = A\star ) into (1) gives

 ((A\star )\star )\star  \cup  (A\star )\star  \cup  A\star  \subseteq  (A\star )\star ,

whence A\star  \subseteq  (A\star )\star .  
Combining the two inclusions yields (A\star )\star  = A\star .

(iii) Monotonicity.  
Assume A \subseteq  B.  Put B (in axiom (1)) equal to A and C equal to B:

 (A\star )\star  \cup  A\star  \cup  B \subseteq  (A \cup  A \cup  B)\star  = B\star .

By (ii) we have (A\star )\star  = A\star , so the left-hand side is simply A\star  \cup  B, which in particular contains A\star .  Hence A\star  \subseteq  B\star , proving monotonicity.

Backward direction: (i)-(iii) \Rightarrow  (1).  
--------------------------------------
Let A, B, C be arbitrary.  We have three easy containments.

1.  A \subseteq  A \cup  B \cup  C, so by (iii) A\star  \subseteq  (A \cup  B \cup  C)\star .  
 With (ii) this yields (A\star )\star  = A\star  \subseteq  (A \cup  B \cup  C)\star .

2.  B \subseteq  A \cup  B \cup  C, so again by (iii) B\star  \subseteq  (A \cup  B \cup  C)\star .

3.  Trivially C \subseteq  A \cup  B \cup  C, hence by (i)  C \subseteq  (A \cup  B \cup  C)\star .

Putting the three inclusions together gives

 (A\star )\star  \cup  B\star  \cup  C \subseteq  (A \cup  B \cup  C)\star ,

which is exactly axiom (1).

Therefore (1) and (i)-(iii) are equivalent, and the cloak operation enjoys inflation, idempotence and monotonicity as required. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Several independent sets (A, B, C) now interact in a single axiom instead of just two; tracking how each of the three pieces behaves under the cloak map forces considerably more bookkeeping.

• Both the cloak of a set (B★) and an uncloaked set (C) appear on the left of the axiom, so the proof must handle the interaction between “closed” and “raw” data simultaneously.

• Deriving idempotence is subtler: it comes from recognising that the same axiom, specialised with B = C = A, simultaneously furnishes two opposite inclusions.  In the original kernel this step required only two sets; here one must keep three coordinated.

• Monotonicity can no longer be read off directly from a single substitution.  One has to choose B and C cunningly (one cloaked, one uncloaked) so that A★ shows up explicitly inside the big union given by the axiom.

• The converse direction likewise demands a triple-layer argument, feeding each of A★, B★ and C into properties (i)–(iii) separately before recombining them.

• Overall the problem contains more variables, more cases to analyse, and a less obvious route to idempotence and monotonicity, making the reasoning chain distinctly longer and more intricate than in either the original problem or the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let S be a non-empty set.  
To every subset A \subseteq  S assign another subset, denoted A\star  and called the cloak of A.  
Assume that for every triple of subsets A, B, C \subseteq  S the following single axiom (the ``triple-cloak inclusion'') holds:

(1)  (A\star )\star  \cup  B\star  \cup  C \subseteq  (A \cup  B \cup  C)\star .

Prove that the cloak operation satisfies  
(i) Inflation    A \subseteq  A\star ;  
(ii) Idempotence  (A\star )\star  = A\star ;  
(iii) Monotonicity  A \subseteq  B \Rightarrow  A\star  \subseteq  B\star .  

Conversely, show that conditions (i)-(iii) alone force the triple-cloak inclusion (1).
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Forward direction: (1) \Rightarrow  (i)-(iii).  
------------------------------------------------
(i) Inflation.  
Put B = C = A in (1):

 (A\star )\star  \cup  A\star  \cup  A \subseteq  (A \cup  A \cup  A)\star  = A\star .

Because the right-hand side contains the whole left-hand side, in particular it contains A; hence A \subseteq  A\star .

(ii) Idempotence.  
With B = C = A again we obtained

 (A\star )\star  \cup  A\star  \cup  A \subseteq  A\star .  (2)

The right-hand side obviously contains A\star ; therefore (2) gives (A\star )\star  \subseteq  A\star .  
On the other hand, plugging A \leftarrow  A\star  (and keeping B = C = A\star ) into (1) gives

 ((A\star )\star )\star  \cup  (A\star )\star  \cup  A\star  \subseteq  (A\star )\star ,

whence A\star  \subseteq  (A\star )\star .  
Combining the two inclusions yields (A\star )\star  = A\star .

(iii) Monotonicity.  
Assume A \subseteq  B.  Put B (in axiom (1)) equal to A and C equal to B:

 (A\star )\star  \cup  A\star  \cup  B \subseteq  (A \cup  A \cup  B)\star  = B\star .

By (ii) we have (A\star )\star  = A\star , so the left-hand side is simply A\star  \cup  B, which in particular contains A\star .  Hence A\star  \subseteq  B\star , proving monotonicity.

Backward direction: (i)-(iii) \Rightarrow  (1).  
--------------------------------------
Let A, B, C be arbitrary.  We have three easy containments.

1.  A \subseteq  A \cup  B \cup  C, so by (iii) A\star  \subseteq  (A \cup  B \cup  C)\star .  
 With (ii) this yields (A\star )\star  = A\star  \subseteq  (A \cup  B \cup  C)\star .

2.  B \subseteq  A \cup  B \cup  C, so again by (iii) B\star  \subseteq  (A \cup  B \cup  C)\star .

3.  Trivially C \subseteq  A \cup  B \cup  C, hence by (i)  C \subseteq  (A \cup  B \cup  C)\star .

Putting the three inclusions together gives

 (A\star )\star  \cup  B\star  \cup  C \subseteq  (A \cup  B \cup  C)\star ,

which is exactly axiom (1).

Therefore (1) and (i)-(iii) are equivalent, and the cloak operation enjoys inflation, idempotence and monotonicity as required. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Several independent sets (A, B, C) now interact in a single axiom instead of just two; tracking how each of the three pieces behaves under the cloak map forces considerably more bookkeeping.

• Both the cloak of a set (B★) and an uncloaked set (C) appear on the left of the axiom, so the proof must handle the interaction between “closed” and “raw” data simultaneously.

• Deriving idempotence is subtler: it comes from recognising that the same axiom, specialised with B = C = A, simultaneously furnishes two opposite inclusions.  In the original kernel this step required only two sets; here one must keep three coordinated.

• Monotonicity can no longer be read off directly from a single substitution.  One has to choose B and C cunningly (one cloaked, one uncloaked) so that A★ shows up explicitly inside the big union given by the axiom.

• The converse direction likewise demands a triple-layer argument, feeding each of A★, B★ and C into properties (i)–(iii) separately before recombining them.

• Overall the problem contains more variables, more cases to analyse, and a less obvious route to idempotence and monotonicity, making the reasoning chain distinctly longer and more intricate than in either the original problem or the current kernel variant.
